skill.name: lamindb
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through LaminDB workflows for biological data management, curation, ontology validation, lineage tracking, integrations, and deployment planning.
skill.summary: Build traceable, queryable biological data workflows with LaminDB guidance.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows the skill is documentation-heavy and the bulk of backtick, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance alerts are false positives from Markdown examples and biology terminology. Medium risk remains because the references include shell commands, sudo examples, cloud credential environment variables, network storage endpoints, and filesystem movement examples that users could apply to real systems. No evidence found of malicious intent, obfuscation, prompt injection, hidden executable scripts, or data exfiltration logic.

medium_findings.title: Shell and Deployment Commands Need Review
medium_findings.description: Static command-execution alerts are mostly Markdown examples, not executable skill code. The setup guide still includes package installation, LaminDB CLI commands, and privileged cache commands that can change a user environment if copied into a shell.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are documentation examples, so direct malicious execution is not present. Risk remains because users may run the commands against real systems.

medium_findings.title: Cloud Credential Examples Require Secret Handling
medium_findings.description: The references show AWS and GCP credential environment variables for LaminDB storage setup. The values are placeholders, but users should not paste real keys into AI prompts or logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Credential variable names and service-account paths are directly present in setup examples. They are legitimate documentation, but mishandling real values would expose secrets.

medium_findings.title: Networked Storage and API Examples Can Move Data
medium_findings.description: The skill documents S3, GCS, S3-compatible endpoints, HTTP artifacts, and a REST API request. These are expected LaminDB integrations, but they involve network access and possible movement of biological data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network examples are explicit and relevant to LaminDB storage integrations. I found no evidence that they exfiltrate data, but real deployments need access review.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Appear False Positive
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptography at many lines, but reviewed locations describe LaminDB concepts, file formats, ontology content, or generic identifiers. No evidence found of a cryptographic implementation or insecure hash use in the skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is documentation context rather than crypto code. Confidence in an actual security threat is very low.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Appear False Positive
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged reconnaissance and network scanning terms, but reviewed examples show ontology lookup, query inspection, or normal troubleshooting commands. No evidence found of scanning hosts, enumerating systems, or collecting environment details for abuse.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious words occur inside legitimate LaminDB or shell troubleshooting documentation. I found no semantic evidence of reconnaissance intent.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Examples Are Legitimate But Need Path Review
low_findings.description: The references include local cache paths, a hidden LaminDB settings directory, temporary export paths, and writing a visualization configuration file. These are normal data-management examples, but users should review paths before running related commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem references are concrete and legitimate. Risk is limited because the skill is not executing them, but copied commands can affect local files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged Shell Commands in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup reference includes sudo commands for shared cache configuration. This is not hidden execution, but privileged commands require human approval before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sudo pattern is explicit in a deployment example. The context is administrative documentation, not malicious code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Environment Variable Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The references include AWS and GCP credential variable examples. Placeholder values reduce immediate risk, but real secrets must stay outside prompts and generated reports.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential names are directly present. The examples appear instructional rather than exfiltrative.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Network Endpoint Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows remote storage and HTTP API endpoint examples. They are expected for LaminDB integrations, but generated advice should treat external endpoints as user-controlled configuration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Network endpoints are visible in examples and align with the skill purpose. I found no evidence of hardcoded malicious destinations.

content.value_statement: Biology teams need traceable datasets, validated annotations, and reproducible workflows. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code plan LaminDB usage across curation, lineage, integrations, and deployment.
actual_capabilities: Explains LaminDB concepts such as artifacts, records, runs, transforms, features, schemas, and collections.
Guides dataset curation with DataFrame, AnnData, MuData, SpatialData, and TileDB-SOMA examples.
Maps biological annotations to ontology resources for genes, proteins, cell types, tissues, diseases, and pathways.
Provides query patterns for filtering, lookup, lineage inspection, streaming, and feature-based discovery.
Covers integrations with Nextflow, Snakemake, Redun, W&B, MLflow, HuggingFace, scVI-tools, DuckDB, and Vitessce.
Summarizes deployment choices for local SQLite, cloud storage, PostgreSQL, AWS S3, GCS, and S3-compatible services.
limitations: It does not install LaminDB or verify package versions for the user.
It cannot validate private datasets unless the user provides schema details and representative metadata.
Cloud, database, and credential examples require review before use in real infrastructure.
The guidance is documentation-based and may need adjustment for local LaminDB versions.

use_cases.title: Curate single-cell datasets
use_cases.description: Plan validation, ontology standardization, artifact registration, and lineage tracking for scRNA-seq or spatial datasets.

use_cases.title: Design reproducible data infrastructure
use_cases.description: Compare local and cloud LaminDB deployment patterns, storage choices, database options, and cache configuration.

use_cases.title: Connect pipelines and experiments
use_cases.description: Map LaminDB into workflow managers, experiment tracking tools, model workflows, and visualization systems.

prompt_templates.title: Start a LaminDB project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a LaminDB project for [dataset type]. Explain the core objects I need and the first safe setup steps.

prompt_templates.title: Validate dataset annotations
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this metadata schema for [assay type]. Suggest LaminDB features, validation rules, and ontology mappings for each field.

prompt_templates.title: Trace a workflow output
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a LaminDB lineage plan for a workflow that starts with [input data] and produces [outputs]. Include runs, transforms, artifacts, and metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Plan production deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare LaminDB deployment options for a team using [cloud provider], [database], and [workflow manager]. Include security and operational tradeoffs.

output_examples.input: Plan LaminDB use for a single-cell RNA-seq atlas.
output: Recommended artifact types for raw, filtered, and annotated AnnData files.
Suggested ontology fields for cell type, tissue, disease, organism, and donor metadata.
Lineage steps for quality control, clustering, annotation, and final atlas release.

output_examples.input: Review deployment options for a small biology team.
output: Local SQLite is suitable for individual development and early prototypes.
Cloud object storage with PostgreSQL is better for shared production use.
Credential handling, cache location, and access controls need explicit team policies.

output_examples.input: Connect LaminDB with MLflow and W&B.
output: Use LaminDB as the source of truth for datasets and lineage.
Use experiment trackers for metrics, model runs, and training metadata.
Record shared identifiers so artifacts, parameters, and results remain linked.

best_practices: Start with minimal required schemas, then add stricter validation after the team agrees on metadata standards.
Use placeholder credentials in prompts and replace them only inside secure local configuration tools.
Track code, data artifacts, parameters, and outputs together so lineage remains reproducible.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real cloud keys, database passwords, or API keys into assistant prompts.
Do not run deployment or sudo commands from generated guidance without reviewing paths and permissions.
Do not treat ontology mappings as final until domain experts review ambiguous biological terms.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants answer LaminDB questions about biological data management, curation, lineage, integrations, and deployment.

faq.question: Does it execute LaminDB commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users decide whether to run commands in their own environment.

faq.question: Which data types does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers DataFrames, AnnData, MuData, SpatialData, TileDB-SOMA, Parquet, Zarr, CSV, and related biological datasets.

faq.question: Can it help with biological ontologies?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains ontology lookup, validation, standardization, hierarchy exploration, and annotation with Bionty resources.

faq.question: Can it design cloud deployments?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares local and cloud patterns, including S3, GCS, PostgreSQL, SQLite, and cache configuration.

faq.question: What should users avoid sharing?
faq.answer: Users should avoid sharing real credentials, private dataset paths, protected health information, or unreleased research data.
